Review

Regulatory mechanisms and breeding strategies for crop drought resistance
=================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Zhenghua He a 1, Pei Zhang b c 1, Haitao Jia a c, Shilong Zhang a, Elsayed Nishawy d e, Xiaopeng Sun a c, Mingqiu Dai b c

Show more

Add to Mendeley

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ncrops.2024.100029](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ncrops.2024.100029 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2949952624000190&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

Abstract
--------

Drought is a primary [abiotic stress](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/abiotic-stress "Learn more about abiotic stress from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") affecting crops, leading to plant stomatal closure, reduced photosynthetic capacity, and reduced yields or even harvest failure. Severe drought can adversely impact agricultural production, ecosystems, and socio-economic capacities. Recently, researchers have studied the regulatory mechanisms of crop [drought resistance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/drought-resistance "Learn more about drought resistance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and cloned hundreds of genes via [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and molecular approaches. However, a limited number of the cloned genes have been successfully employed in drought resistance breeding, suggesting that drought resistance regulation is too complex. More work must be done to fully understand the regulatory networks of drought responses to breed drought-resistant and high-yield crop varieties. This review outlines the current achievements in investigating crop drought responses, particularly regulation by phytohormones and regulation of genes at transcriptional, post-translational, and epigenetic levels in crop drought responses. Finally, we examine the problems and potential solutions in breeding crop drought resistance and propose strategies for crop drought resistance improvement.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000189)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000207)

Keywords
--------

Crops

Drought resistance

Regulation mechanism

Genetic improvement

Introduction
------------

In the past four decades (1964–2007), the loss of cereal crop yield triggered by drought was about 1800 million Mg, equivalent to the total global production of maize and wheat in 2013 ([Lesk et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib60)). As most plants cannot avoid drought and environmental stresses through movement, complex responsive networks have been evolved. Plants sense and transmit drought stress signals via changes in the concentrations of plant hormones, ions, and [small molecules](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/small-molecule "Learn more about small molecules from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") from internal and external environments, which are perceived by transcription factors to regulate drought-responsive gene expression and help the plants respond to drought. Recently, researchers have obtained a more detailed understanding of crop drought responses. Hundreds of genes influencing crop [drought resistance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/drought-resistance "Learn more about drought resistance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have been identified and functionally verified ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#fig0005)). In addition, many studies have demonstrated a diversity of drought resistance in different cultivars of crops, encompassing maize, rice, and wheat, enabling the dissection of the [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") regulatory networks. Other methods to improve crop drought resistance in the absence of genetic improvement have demonstrated great future potential application value. In this study, we introduced mechanisms of crop drought responses at various levels, including regulation by phytohormones and regulation through alternative transcription, post-translational modifications, and [epigenetic modifications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/epigenetic-modification "Learn more about epigenetic modifications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). We outlined the progress in improving crop drought resistance with or without genetic methods. In addition, as the limitations in using genes affect plant drought resistance, we discussed the current issues and potential solutions for breeding crops with drought resistance.

![Image 9: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2949952624000190-gr1.jpg)

Fig. 1. **Annual numbers of genes verified to affect drought resistance of major crops****.**

Regulatory mechanisms of crop drought responses
-----------------------------------------------

### Phytohormones

Various phytohormones such as auxins, [abscisic acid](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/abscisic-acid "Learn more about abscisic acid from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (ABA), [brassinosteroids](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/brassinosteroid "Learn more about brassinosteroids from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (BRs), cytokinins (CKs), ethylene (ET), gibberellins (GAs), [jasmonic acid](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/jasmonate "Learn more about jasmonic acid from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (JA), and strigolactones (SL) regulate plant drought responses. When drought occurs, the transmission of phytohormonal signals causes changes in the expression of drought-responsive genes and numerous physiological responses and eventually assists plants in adapting to drought via different strategies, including drought avoidance, tolerance, escape, and recovery ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#fig0010)).

![Image 10: Fig. 2](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2949952624000190-gr2.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (351KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2949952624000190-gr2_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (351KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2949952624000190-gr2.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 2. **Phytohormones regulate plant physiological responses via cascade signaling pathways and allow drought adaptation through different strategies.**

Ⅰ/Ⅱ/Ⅲ/Ⅳ/Ⅴ/Ⅵ represent different levels of hormone signal transduction pathways according to studies on _Arabidopsis_, while the genes from various crops involved in hormone signaling pathways are shown below.

ABA is the primary phytohormone that regulates plant stress responses. In response to drought stress, endogenous ABA levels rise rapidly, and ABA receptors (RCARs/PYR/PYLs) inhibit the activity of [protein phosphatase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/phosphoprotein-phosphatase "Learn more about protein phosphatase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") PP2CAs by producing a complex with ABA and PP2CAs. SnRK2s, which originally bind to [PP2C proteins](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/protein-c "Learn more about PP2C proteins from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), are released and activated by [autophosphorylation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/autophosphorylation "Learn more about autophosphorylation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or other kinases to modulate ion channel proteins, transcription factors, and a variety of transporters so that plants can respond to external drought stress. ABA receptors, including _ZmPYL8_/_9_/_12_ in maize and _OsPYL3_/_5_/_6_/_7_/_9_/_10_ in rice, positively regulate drought responses ([He et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib38), [Bhatnagar et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib4), [Santosh Kumar et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib110)). _ZmPP2C-A10_, a member of the maize PP2CA phosphatase family, is a negative regulator of drought response, and its over-expression produced drought hypersensitivity in both maize and _[Arabidopsis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/arabidopsis "Learn more about Arabidopsis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ ([Xiang et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib138)). [SnRK2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/protein-kinases "Learn more about SnRK2 from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[family genes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/multigene-family "Learn more about family genes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in wheat, such as _TaSnRK2.3_/_4_/_7_/_8_/_9_, positively regulate drought responses ([Feng et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib26)). The transmission of ABA signals can trigger various [physiological processes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/physiological-process "Learn more about physiological processes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), including stomatal closure, root system modulation, and organizing [soil microbial communities](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/soil-microflora "Learn more about soil microbial communities from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which help plants survive drought ([Muhammad Aslam et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib96)).

Ethylene (ET) is a two-carbon gas operating as a phytohormone and is widely involved in growth, development, and stress responses. When ethylene is absent, ethylene receptors (including ETR1) can interact with CTR1 and activate its kinase activity. Activated CTR1 inhibits ethylene signaling through phosphorylation of EIN2 at its [C terminal domain](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/c-terminus "Learn more about C terminal domain from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (EIN2-CEND). When ethylene is produced, binding to the receptor protein leads to conformational changes that block the activation of CTR1, leading to the [dephosphorylation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/dephosphorylation "Learn more about dephosphorylation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of EIN2. Dephosphorylated EIN2-CEND undergoes cleavage from the [ER membrane](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/endoplasmic-reticulum-membrane "Learn more about ER membrane from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and is transported into the nucleus, which promotes the binding of transcription factor EIN3 to DNA by prompting [histone acetylation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/histone-acetylation "Learn more about histone acetylation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and activates the ethylene pathway ([Zhao et al., 2021a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib164)). Previous research has revealed that activation of ethylene [biosynthesis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/anabolism "Learn more about biosynthesis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [signaling pathways](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/signal-transduction "Learn more about signaling pathways from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") enhances crop [drought resistance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/drought-resistance "Learn more about drought resistance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Overexpression of _OsETOL1_ and _OsARD1_ enhanced ethylene synthesis and drought resistance in rice ([Du et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib24), [Liang et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib71)). Ethylene response factors (ERFs) operate as plant-specific transcription factors in the AP2 / ERF gene family. Members of the ERF family across multiple crops affect drought resistance, such as _ZmERF21_ in maize, _OsERF3/48/71/83/101/109_ in rice, _GmERF135_ in soybean, and _TaERF3_ in wheat ([Rong et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib108), [Yu et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib145), [Jin et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib47), [Li et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib65), [Zhao et al., 2019b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib163), [Jung et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib50), [Wang et al., 2022b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib134), [Yu et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib146)). When plants experience drought, they produce increased levels of ethylene to promote leaf [abscission](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/abscission "Learn more about abscission from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and reduce transpiration area and [water loss](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/water-loss "Learn more about water loss from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") rate, to survive drought.

Brassinosteroids (BRs) are a class of polyhydroxylated plant steroid hormones. Growing evidence shows that they have important roles in plant growth, development, and stress responses, including extreme temperature and drought stresses. When BRs are present, the BRI1 and BAK1 receptor complexes activate downstream protein kinases, namely BSK and CDG1, which then activate protein phosphatase BSU1. Activated BSU1 inhibits protein kinase BIN2 by dephosphorylation, and substrates of BIN2, like transcription factor BES1/BZR1, are subsequently dephosphorylated by protein phosphatase 2 A (PP2A). Dephosphorylated BES1/BZR1 proteins modulate the transcription of downstream genes and transmit the BR signal ([Nolan et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib101)). In wheat, _TaBZR2_ positively regulates drought responses by activating _TaGST1,_ functioning positively in scavenging drought-induced superoxide anions (O 2-) in wheat ([Cui et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib17)). Overexpression of _ZmBES1_/_BZR1‐5_ in _Arabidopsis_ enhanced drought resistance through regulation of stem growth, root development, and metabolite pathways ([Sun et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib118)).

Auxin was the first identified phytohormone involved in numerous processes like [cell differentiation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cellular-differentiation "Learn more about cell differentiation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), formation of root morphology, root [gravitropism](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/gravitropism "Learn more about gravitropism from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), seed germination, and floral [organ development](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/organogenesis "Learn more about organ development from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). When plants sense auxin, the F-box protein TIR1/AFB binds to transcriptional inhibitors AUX/IAA, leading to their degradation and relieving ARF transcription factor inhibition. Activated ARFs transmit the auxin signal by regulating numerous downstream genes ([Lavy and Estelle, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib56)). When exogenous auxin is administered, the expression levels of drought-responsive genes, such as _RAB18_, _RD22_, and _DREB2A_, are significantly upregulated. Additionally, the number of lateral roots and plant root structures is also enhanced. These molecular and growth responses suggest that auxin positively regulates plant drought responses ([Salvi et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib109)). _OsIAA6_, _OsIAA18_, and _OsIAA20_ in rice impact drought resistance by regulating plant growth and development, reactive oxygen species clearance, and stomatal closure, respectively ([Jung et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib51), [Wang et al., 2021a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib128), [Zhang et al., 2021a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib154)).

Gibberellins (GAs) are a class of tetracyclic diterpenoid phytohormones that regulate various aspects of plant development, including seed germination, [stem elongation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/stem-elongation "Learn more about stem elongation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), leaf expansion, pollen maturation, and the development of flowers, fruits, and seeds. When GA is present, the GA receptor GID binds GA, promoting the interaction between GID and DELLA transcriptional inhibitor proteins. The DELLA proteins are ubiquitinated by the [E3 ubiquitin ligase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/ubiquitin-conjugating-enzyme "Learn more about E3 ubiquitin ligase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") SCF and degraded by the 26 S [proteasome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/proteasome "Learn more about proteasome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), relieving transcriptional inhibition of downstream genes and activating the GA signaling pathway ([Gao and Chu, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib29)). Studies have demonstrated that a decrease in GA content and activity can enhance plant drought resistance. _GAMT1_ encodes a [methyltransferase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/methyltransferase "Learn more about methyltransferase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which can convert GA into an inactivated GA. Overexpression of _AtGAMT1_ in tomato enhanced drought resistance ([Nir et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib99)). GIBBERELLIN SUPPRESSING FACTOR (GSF) negatively regulates gibberellin biosynthesis by regulating the expression levels of GA deactivation genes like _GA2-oxidases 2_/_6_ (_GA2ox2_/_6_) and GA biosynthetic genes including _GA20-oxidases 1–4_ (_GA20ox1–4_). Overexpressing GSF in _Arabidopsis_ enhanced drought resistance ([Chen et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib13)). The [cytochrome P450](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/cytochrome-p450 "Learn more about cytochrome P450 from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") family member _CYP71D8L_ reduced endogenous GA levels and enhanced drought resistance in rice ([Zhou et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib167)).

Strigolactones (SL) are carotenoid-derived phytohormones that regulate [biological processes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/biological-phenomena-and-functions-concerning-the-entire-organism "Learn more about biological processes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), encompassing plant growth, development, and stress responses. Strigolactone binding by the α/β-hydrolase receptor D14 prompts the formation of a complex with D3/MAX2 and SCF proteins, and the complex then promotes the degradation of strigolactone inhibitor D53/SMXL6/7/8 through [ubiquitination](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/ubiquitination "Learn more about ubiquitination from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), activating downstream gene expression ([Mostofa et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib95), [Bhoi et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib5)). Studies have demonstrated that genes in strigolactone biosynthesis and signaling pathways are involved in plant drought responses. In rice, mutants of SL synthesis-related genes _D3, D10_, and _D17_ showed increased resistance to drought, while the _D27_ mutant was more sensitive to drought ([Haider et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib34)). These findings indicate diverse roles of genes involved in strigolactone biosynthesis and signaling in the regulation of crop drought responses, however, more studies are required.

Cytokinins (CKs) are a group of plant hormones arising from [adenine](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/adenine "Learn more about adenine from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which are involved in various biological processes like cell differentiation, root and aerial segment formation, [leaf senescence](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/leaf-senescence "Learn more about leaf senescence from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and chloroplast development. CKs are associated with various [abiotic stress](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/abiotic-stress "Learn more about abiotic stress from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") responses, including drought responses. The CK signaling pathway in _Arabidopsis_ includes a multi-step His-Asp phosphorylation process. The CK receptors [histone](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/histone "Learn more about histone from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") kinases AHK2/3/4 and phosphotransfer proteins AHP1–5 participate in this process and transmit signals to responsive factors (ARRs), which regulate the expression of downstream genes ([Li et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib66), [Li et al., 2021b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib69)). Studies have demonstrated that CK negatively regulates plant drought resistance as mutations in _AHK2_/_3_, _AHP2_/_3_/_5_, and _ARR1_/_10_/_12_ improved plant drought resistance ([Kang et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib52), [Nishiyama et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib100), [Nguyen et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib97)). Notably, the effects of CK on plant drought resistance vary. For instance, overexpression of the CK [synthesis gene](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/gene-synthesis "Learn more about synthesis gene from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_IPT1_ in cassava and _TaIPT_ in wheat enhanced plant drought resistance ([Zhang et al., 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib151), [Beznec et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949952624000190#bib3)).

Jasmonic acid (JA) is a lipid-derived phytohormone n

SUMMARY:
Furthermore, application of crop development regulators; preservation of membrane integrity; and use of appropriate plant genotypes, antioxidants, compatible solutes, stress-related proteins, and aquaporins support the development of drought-tolerant crops [24]. Optimization of CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing to modify abiotic stress responses in plants. Down Regulation and Loss of Auxin Response Factor 4 Function Using CRISPR/Cas9 Alters Plant Growth, Stomatal Function and Improves Tomato Tolerance to Salinity and Osmotic Stress. Reduced drought tolerance by CRISPR/Cas9-mediated SlMAPK3 mutagenesis in tomato plants. Genome editing using CRISPR/Cas9–targeted mutagenesis: an opportunity for yield improvements of crop plants grown under environmental stresses. Ethylene response factor (ERF) family proteins in abiotic stresses and CRISPR–Cas9 genome editing of ERFs for multiple abiotic stress tolerance in crop plants: a review.

CRISPR–Cas9-based genetic engineering for crop improvement under drought stress
===============================================================================

PMCID: PMC8808358 PMID: [34506262](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34506262/)

ABSTRACT
--------

In several parts of the world, the prevalence and severity of drought are predicted to increase, creating considerable pressure on global agricultural yield. Among all abiotic stresses, drought is anticipated to produce the most substantial impact on soil biota and plants, along with complex environmental impacts on other ecological systems. Being sessile, plants tend to be the least resilient to drought-induced osmotic stress, which reduces nutrient accessibility due to soil heterogeneity and limits nutrient access to the root system. Drought tolerance is a complex quantitative trait regulated by multiple genes, and it is one of the most challenging characteristics to study and classify. Fortunately, the clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeat (CRISPR) technology has paved the way as a new frontier in crop improvement, thereby revolutionizing plant breeding. The application of CRISPER systems has proven groundbreaking across numerous biological fields, particularly in biomedicine and agriculture. The present review highlights the principle and optimization of CRISPR systems and their implementation for crop improvement, particularly in terms of drought tolerance, yield, and domestication. Furthermore, we address the ways in which innovative genome editing tools can help recognize and modify novel genes coffering drought tolerance. We anticipate the establishment of effective strategies of crop yield improvement in water-limited regions through collaborative efforts in the near future.

**KEYWORDS:** Drought, CRISPR–Cas9, plant productivity, ABA regulation, ethylene, genome editing

Introduction
------------

In 2017, at least 3% of the world’s land area was affected by extreme drought [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0001)]. Approximately a fifth of the world’s population does not have sufficient food to survive normally, and nearly a billion people are hungry every year [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0001),[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0002)]. Drought decreases agricultural productivity, contributing to food shortage, and it is therefore one of the major causes of undernourishment and hunger [[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0002)]. In the mid-twentieth century, drought was the key reason of the scarcity of world grain production relative to demand, causing the food security crisis [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0003)]. Farmers suffer from economic losses when crops are damaged by drought. Ranchers must spend additional money on food and water for their livestock. Business that relies on agriculture, such as companies producing tractors and food, experience losses when drought threatens crops or livestock. Severe drought reduces crop yield and production because of the scarcity of water and soil moisture to support plant growth. Under drought conditions, farmers reduce the area under crop cultivation and only plant drought-tolerant crops [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0004)].

Impact of climate extremes on crop production
---------------------------------------------

Agricultural food production is directly affected by climatic factors, such as rainfall and temperature. These factors regulate crop development and health, crop yield over time, and annual crop yield [[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0005)]. Environmental extremes are estimated to become more frequent due to climate change, which can adversely affect crop yield [[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0007)]. While the impacts of climate change on agriculture have been reported at various regional scales, adaptive improvements to advance cropping practices for mitigating the effects of drought on plant yield have never been explored [[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0007)]. In addition, the impacts of weather extremes on the yield and growth of rainfed and irrigated plants warrant extensive research, given that groundwater, a vital resource for irrigation during drought, is slowly depleting. Severe drought decreases plant yield because less water and soil moisture are accessible for plant development. Under drought conditions, farmers deliberately reduce area under crop cultivation, and only plant drought-tolerant plants. Nevertheless, the spatiotemporal variability of the effects of drought on yield must be considered to mitigate and minimize possible negative impact on plant production [[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0008)].

Negative effects of drought stress on plant activity
----------------------------------------------------

Water scarcity elicits various crop responses at the physiological, molecular, biochemical, and morphological levels, eventually interrupting crop productively [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0009)]. As shown in [Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#f0001), drought stress reduces plant production at various stages of growth. Indeed, reduced water supply decreases the germination and growth rate of crops [[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0010)]. During plant growth, drought affects plant water–interactions, which in turn disrupt the entire metabolism (at the molecular and physiological level), depending on the magnitude and duration of stress [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0011),[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0012)]. In addition, water scarcity alters plant processes, like suppression of photosynthesis [[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0013),[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0014)] and reduction of crop productivity [[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0015),[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0016)] are the major consequences. Under water deficit, indirect or direct oxidative stress serves as one of the key drivers of plant response, resulting in the alteration of biochemical machinery and destruction of cell membrane integrity, which further lead to severe metabolic complaints ultimately affecting plant function [[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0017),[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0018)].

### Figure 1.

![Image 9: Figure 1.](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/9f83/8808358/6dface1ef189/KBIE_A_1969831_F0001_OC.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/figure/f0001/)

Drought stress significantly suppresses plant growth and development

Drought stress as a limiting factor for plant development
---------------------------------------------------------

Drought is known as a limiting factor for several aspects of plant production. Germination, coleoptile length, and plant health are the key aspects of crop development [[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0019)]. Germination is a crucial step during development, and it is susceptible to drought. Notable changes in the seed germination of several crops, including wheat, sorghum, and maize, have been reported [[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0020),[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0021)]. The noticeable signs of plants subject to drought stress at the early stages of vegetative growth include the disturbance of flowering, reduction in plant height, and wilting of leaves [[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0022)]. Drought often limits nutrient absorption of crops because of insufficient soil humidity, thereby suppressing stem development. In _Lathyrus sativus_, shoot length was reduced under water scarcity [[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0023)]. As a limiting factor, drought affects a wide spectrum of plant physiological processes, including growth and metabolism. Timing, duration, degree, and rate of development are critical factors taken into account during the selection of drought-tolerant species. Further, drought adversely affects several plant biological processes, from the embryonic to reproductive and maturation stages.

Plant drought tolerance mechanisms involve multiple biological processes at the level of cells, organs, and whole plant, which are activated at different plant developmental stages. For instance, plants reduce water loss through effective stomatal conductance and increase water uptake and transfer through the development of a highly efficient and deep root system ([Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#f0001)). Furthermore, application of crop development regulators; preservation of membrane integrity; and use of appropriate plant genotypes, antioxidants, compatible solutes, stress-related proteins, and aquaporins support the development of drought-tolerant crops [[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0024)]. Moreover, plants with improved water use efficiency and enhanced antioxidant apparatus as well as those that can produce major osmolites and secondary metabolites are suitable as material to develop drought-tolerant crops. In addition, under water-limited conditions, compounds that can enhance drought tolerance of plants can be exogenously applied. Biotechnological approaches to produce drought-tolerant transgenic plants should also be considered, although their validity cannot precede the current field trials.

Molecular mechanisms underlying plant drought tolerance
-------------------------------------------------------

Improving agronomic traits that offer plant tolerance/resistance to abiotic and biotic stresses to enhance their economic worth has long been a global concern. Knowledge of global warming and climate change underlines the need to incorporate some practical and safe strategies. In some countries, the sustainability of crop production during drought is a significant problem. Drought intensity varies both temporally and spatially. Plants have evolved sophisticated responses and developed numerous physiological and morphological strategies to withstand stress. Specifically, plants face drought with differing degrees of adaptation, prevention, and escape [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0004),[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0021)]. The exploitation of genetic traits that improve drought response while retaining high yield remains crucial for plant management. Transgenic and conventional breeding methods could enhance the drought tolerance of wheat, soyabean, rice, and maize.

In the past, conventional breeding was the most productive way of growing plants, which encouraged their development in water-limited environments. However, these methods are labor-intestine, time-consuming, and expensive. Under stress conditions, molecular markers have played a pivotal role in characterizing the large variability in plant genetics [[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0019)]. In various crops, numerous quantitative trait loci (QTLs) conferring drought tolerance have been identified [[25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0025),[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0026)]. However, the accuracy and reliability of QTL identification remain problematic. In this light, genetic engineering has proven very successful in improving crops against abiotic and biotic stresses [[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0027)].

Innovative technologies that can expand the stress responses of plants and make them environment-ready are warranted. The introduction of genome editing tools has realized significant advances in plant breeding. Genome editing tools use sequence-definite nucleases to introduce specific known variations in the genome [[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0028)]. The CRISPR–Cas system of genome editing has been extensively acknowledged for its adaptability and ease of operation. This strategy uses a single guide RNA and complex Cas endonuclease that changes along the DNA strand to produce a double-stranded DNA breaks. Subsequently, these breaks are repaired by endogenic cell mending, leading to the expansion of novel mutations [[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0029),[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0030)]. The CRISPR–Cas system has been professionally used for achieving resistance to multiple stresses, including heavy metals, salinity, drought, and submergence [[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0031),[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0032)]. The present review emphases on the application of the CRISPR–Cas9 system to achieve drought tolerance in plants and discusses the potential of this technology in the expansion of drought-tolerant plant varieties.

Inclusive molecular studies have interpreted cellular pathways regulating plant response to drought [[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0033),[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0034)]. Abscisic acid (ABA) plays crucial roles in regulating plant response to drought by controlling stomatal closure and gene expression to limit water loss via transpiration [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0011)]. Basic leucine zipper (bZIP) transcription factor, called ABA-responsive element (ABRE)-binding proteins is essential for ABA signaling [[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0033)]. In soybean, rice, and _Arabidopsis, AREB1_ over-expression (_ABF2_) improved drought tolerance, while _AREB1_ loss-of-function increased drought susceptibility [[35–39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0035)]. Furthermore, under drought, _AREB1_ controls a broad range of genes downstream of the ABA signaling pathway [[39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0039)] and is involved in ABA-mediated antioxidant signaling, ABA biosynthesis, and osmotic stress response. Therefore, _AREB1_ is an attractive candidate to enhance plant response to drought [[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0036),[40–42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0040)].

Accessibility to genome sequences of many plants and advances in genome editing methodologies have opened new avenues of breeding for several desirable traits. Developments in genome editing tools, such as transcription activator-like effector nucleases (TALENs) and zinc-finger nucleases (ZFNs), have allowed molecular biologists to more specifically target any gene of interest. Nevertheless, these approaches are costly and laborious, since they need complex phases involving protein engineering. Unlike first-generation genome editing approaches, the CRISPR–Cas9 system requires easy operation and simple cloning procedures. The same Cas9 is tentatively available for use with various guide RNAs, targeting several sites in the genome. Following proof-of-concept demonstrations using a preliminary CRISPR–Cas9 unit in plants, numerous types of Cas9 cassettes (StCas9, SaCas9, and NmCas9) have been introduced to enhance target accuracy and reduce off-target cleavage. Furthermore, the availability of Cas9 enzymes from other bacteria has increased the accuracy and efficiency of gene editing [[43](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0043),[44](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0044)]. The present review outlines the options open to agro-biotechnologists for crop enhancement using the established Cas9-based genome editing techniques. Cas9 enzymes have been used to enhance abiotic and biotic stress tolerance/resistance [[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0027),[45](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0045)]. Implementation of these strategies is expected to produce non-genetically modified (non-GMO) plants with the objective phenotype, which may improve yield under abiotic and biotic stresses [[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0021)].

CRISPR-based targeted genome editing for drought tolerance
----------------------------------------------------------

Various abiotic stresses substantially reduce crop yield by suppressing plant growth and reducing plant productivity [[46](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0046)]. Due to the dynamic nature of drought stress, genomic adaptation has previously been shown to be the sole approach to achieve drought tolerance. Overexpression of many genes and transcription factors related to drought signaling promote the aggregation of signaling molecules and metabolites and improve crop drought tolerance [[47](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0047)]. Meanwhile, the expressions of sensitive (S) genes increase plant drought susceptibility via hormonal imbalance, low antioxidant activity, and reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation. Stress-related ring finger protein 1 (OsSRFP1), drought-induced SINA protein 1 (OsDIS1), and dry- and salt-tolerant protein 1 (OsDST) are negative regulators of drought tolerance, whose silencing increased antioxidant enzyme levels, reduced H 2 O 2 concentrations, and enhanced drought tolerance in rice [[47](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0047),[48](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0048)]. Natural drought tolerance can be realized via genome editing to target drought-sensitive genes or negative regulators of abiotic stress response. As the first proof-of-concept study, the CRISPR–Cas9 system was used to introduce novel alleles in _Arabidopsis_ OPEN STOMATA 2 (OST2)-encoding gene – a key plasma membrane H+ ATPase crucial for stomatal function [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0011)]. Plasma membrane H+ ATPases are involved in the generation of proton gradient to initiate stomatal conductance [[42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0042)]. In the presence of dehydration, ABA binds to the C-terminus of the proton pump, suppressing H+ ATPase function and inducing stomatal closure. Importantly, two significant mutations at the ost2 locus obliterate stomatal response to ABA, contributing to the constitutive functioning of proton pumps and induction of necrotic lesions [[42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0042)]. Using an efficient CRISPR–Cas framework combining Cas9 and a truncated sgRNA (tru-sgRNA), mutations in transgenic crops could be identified with extraordinary efficacy (>32%) and without off-target alteration. Assessment of stomatal response under ABA-arbitrated conditions showed that compared with the wild-type, the _ost2_-CRISPR mutant exhibited a substantially higher rate of stomatal closure coupled with a lower rate of transcriptional water loss ([Figure 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#f0002), [Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#t0001)). Therefore, CRISPR–Cas9-mediated mutations at the OST2 locus enhanced drought tolerance by improving stomatal response. Most recently, the CRISPR–Cas9 system was used to produce mutant lines of the non-expresser tomato of pathogenesis-related 1 (_NPR1_) to validate the role of this gene in drought tolerance [[49](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0049)]. Although _NPR1_ is a key stimulator of the plant defense system, its function in abiotic stress is negligible. However, in drought-responsive apple plants, _MdNPR1_ downregulation has been documented [[50](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0050)]. In rice, _AtNPR1_ overexpression resulted in a hypersensitive response to drought stress (Li et al. [51](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0051)). In wild tomato, CRISPR–Cas9-mediated loss-of-function _s1npr1_ mutants exhibited weaker drought tolerance, higher stomatal opening rate, higher malondialdehyde (MDA) content, greater electrolytic leakage, and higher H 2 O 2 levels, and lower antioxidant enzyme levels. Moreover, down regulation of drought-responsive genes, including _SIDREB, SIDHN_, and _SIGST_, further confirmed the drought susceptibility of _s1npr1_ mutants ([Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#t0001)). Consequently, in tomatoes and other crops, _S1NPR1_ plays critical roles in regulating drought response, and multiple _SlNPR1_ variants can be produced using genome editing to confer broad-spectrum drought tolerance [[49](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0049)].

### Figure 2.

![Image 10: Figure 2.](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/9f83/8808358/40c34947a12d/KBIE_A_1969831_F0002_OC.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/figure/f0002/)

CRISPR–Cas9 alleviates drought stress and promotes plant growth and development

### Table 1.

Application of the CRISPR-based genome editing approach in plants for improvement of drought stress tolerance

| Species | Target gene/site | Transformation method/Strategy | Target trait/Improved trait | Reference |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| _Arabidopsis_ | Open stomata 2 (OST2) | Agrobacterium-mediated transformation | Drought stress tolerance by altering stomatal closing | [[52](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0052)] |
| _Arabidopsis_ | miR169a | Agrobacterium-mediated transformation/ dual-sgRNA/Cas9-mediated targeted deletion to create null mutations | Targeting sensitive gene replacement by HDR | [[53](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0053)] |
| Maize | Auxin-regulated gene involved in organ size [ARGOS] | Biolistic mediated transformation/ CRISPR/Cas9-mediated DNA repair in untranslated regions of target genes to produce over expression | Overexpression of ARGOS8 to reduce ethylene sensitivity to enhance flowering/ increase grain yield under drought stress | [[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0032)] |
| _Arabidopsis_ | Arabidopsis thaliana vacuolar H+- pyrophosphatase [AVP1] | Agrobacterium-mediated transformation/CRISPR/Cas9 activation system to produce overexpression of target gene | Enhanced number of leaves and leaf area | [[54](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/#cit0054)] |
| _Arabidopsis_ | ABA-responsive element- binding protein 1 (AREB1) | Agrobacterium-mediated transformation/CRISPR activation system to enhance the expression of target gene | Increased chlorophyll cornetts and faster stomatal opening | [[55](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808358/

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 5: Drought-Resistant Crops ---
URL: https://www.interesjournals.org/articles/droughtresistant-crops-a-sustainable-solution-to-climate-change-111271.html

SUMMARY:
These crops are either naturally evolved to thrive in arid climates or are genetically modified to enhance their tolerance to drought.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- IMAGES ---

IMAGE 1:
URL: https://image.slidesharecdn.com/droughttoleranceincrops-200721073701/85/drought-tolerance-in-crops-1-638.jpg?cb=1667328419
DESCRIPTION: The image illustrates the mechanisms by which genetically modified (GM) crops exhibit drought tolerance, featuring diagrams of plant responses to water scarcity, the role of specific genes like EPSPS and nitrogen usage, and highlighting the contributions of Vikas Verma, a Ph.D. research scholar.

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/483633/fpls-10-01676-HTML-r1/image_m/fpls-10-01676-g002.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The illustration depicts a maize plant with various annotations highlighting genetic techniques and aspects, such as Hahb4 for reduced ethylene sensitivity, CspA and CspB for high chlorophyll content, NF-YB1 and NF-YB2 for high stomatal conductance, and factors related to carbon allocation and root characteristics to enhance crop resistance to drought and floods.

IMAGE 3:
URL: https://image.slidesharecdn.com/droughttoleranceincrops-200721073701/75/Drought-tolerance-in-crops-4-2048.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The image illustrates the progression of drought impacts, including meteorological, agricultural, hydrological, and socioeconomic droughts, highlighting how reduced water availability affects soil, crops, water bodies, wildlife, and human communities over time.

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://www.preprints.org/img/dyn_abstract_figures/2019/12/bfec249126bb15f96d76dcee408e0af8/Screenshot+(28).png
DESCRIPTION: None

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/483633/fpls-10-01676-HTML-r1/image_m/fpls-10-01676-g003.jpg
DESCRIPTION: A diagram illustrates a framework for enhancing crop resilience to drought and floods, showcasing processes such as the translation of promising traits, development of stress response promoters for monocots, analysis of drought responses, experimental design in Arabidopsis, and the use of HTTP for corroborating results and testing novel crop genes.

enriched_points.point_title: Explore GM crops for drought tolerance
enriched_points.expanded_content: Investigate genetically modified (GM) crops designed to withstand drought by enhancing their physiological traits, thus improving agricultural resilience under water-scarce conditions.
Examine the biological pathways and genetic factors, such as phytohormones and gene regulation, that enable crops to cope with drought-induced stress.
Present successful examples of drought-tolerant GM crops, showcasing their development, performance in the field, and impact on food security.
Explore emerging technologies like CRISPR/Cas9 for developing advanced drought-resistant crops and the potential for improved yields and adaptability.
Identify hurdles in GM crop adoption, such as regulatory concerns and public perception, while proposing solutions to enhance acceptance and implementation.
